annotations.text: the leading diagnostic possibilities are: (1) a high-grade glioma, particularly an IDH-wildtype glioblastoma with unusual imaging features; (2) neurosarcoidosis; and (3) primary CNS lymphoma.

Given all of this, the leading diagnostic possibilities are: (1) a high-grade glioma, particularly an IDH-wildtype glioblastoma with unusual imaging features; (2) neurosarcoidosis; and (3) primary CNS lymphoma.

The olfactory hallucinations, in particular, are a classic ictal or interictal phenomenon arising from the uncus or amygdala, regions of the medial temporal lobe that are part of the primary olfactory cortex.

Their persistence over a year, coupled with the development of sensory symptoms and progressive neuroimaging abnormalities, strongly suggests an underlying structural lesion with epileptogenic and infiltrative properties, rather than a functional or psychiatric disorder.
Gliomas, particularly those of lower grade but with infiltrative potential, must be at the top of the differential.
The location—medial right temporal lobe extending into the insula and basal ganglia—is classic for a diffuse astrocytoma, particularly an IDH-mutant type.
The absence of systemic symptoms—no fever, weight loss, rash, joint pain, or respiratory complaints—makes systemic inflammatory or infectious processes less likely, though not impossible.
The normal magnetic resonance spectroscopy is an important negative finding: elevated choline, reduced N-acetylaspartate, and the presence of lactate or lipid peaks are common in high-grade gliomas.

the pattern of leptomeningeal nodularity, the location in the basal ganglia and brainstem, the patient’s demographic profile, and the absence of typical neoplastic imaging features (e.g., extensive edema, necrosis, pronounced spectroscopic abnormalities) make neurosarcoidosis the most plausible and diagnostically urgent consideration.

The fact that the patient is on dextroamphetamine, which can cause CNS hyperexcitability, does not explain the structural lesion or the leptomeningeal spread, but it may have masked or exacerbated subtle symptoms.
The patient’s age and ethnicity make sarcoidosis more plausible, as it is more common in African Americans and typically presents between ages 20 and 40.
